Hey, welcome back for your first lesson on standards. Sarah starts digging in with big picture questions first. Makes sense, right? She asked me, what is the origin of my own really high standards? You have an incredibly high standard. Was I born with that? Did I develop it over time? And if so, how was to learn from that? Check out this first interview here. This first question, you can be blown away. It's really great stuff. And I will see you on the other side. So you seem to have developed high standards at a very young age. Do you think that you are an exception to the rule or are most people like you? I think, again, that it depends on standards for what. We look at people in public school. There were certain kids in my class. There was one girl named Monica stored there. I'll never forget her. Monica was like my nemesis because we were the two kids that always had the highest test scores, right? But Monica was like this really studious. She had a hair bun even better than yours, by the way. She was this unbelievably studious girl, right? I wasn't that studious, but I did really well in school. It came easy to me. I would say her standard for academic excellence was incredibly high. Higher than mine. I just naturally did well on tests and stuff. So it came easy to me. So we see people with standards at very young ages. So we say, okay, that person's going to really high standard for academics. We might see another kid who at the age of 13 or 14 is already playing six hours of golfer day and trying to become a scratch golfer who wants to go pro, a kid on the football team. We see a girl who's a cheerleader who's practicing at Shares Non. So I think you start to see as people transition from being under the age of 89, they come into adolescence. You start seeing how people's beliefs and values start taking shape and form who they are as people. Then the standards get good. So look at it this way. And I think I mentioned this in the introduction to this module. I said that standards are the sort of long term indicator of success while your state is the short term indicator. Now we've already been through the state management module, so everyone gets that. In other words, if you don't show up to the sale or to any situation in an empowered state, well, you really can't access the resources that you have. You're sort of blocked. So you could be the greatest salesperson, the best parent, the best anything with those resources are locked inside you. They can't flow out. When you're in an empowered state, what happens is a spicket opens and you can be your best self. And we never see that anywhere more parented in sports with world-class athletes when they're also great at what they do. So to raise their thin differences, yet why are some like the best of the best because they can harness that, bring it to the table day and day out, be in their best state. So that's that in the moment indicator. But what people don't really focus enough on is the fact that where you end up in life is not dictated by your state. That's like you're in the moment, can I bring it to the table on one specific day versus my standard, the long-term thermostat. Like where do you burn? What I found the strat, and this was really important, was the first breakthrough I made in terms of managing people with the inner game of the straight line. Actually, I said it's a second breakthrough. The first one was the state manager. I knew right away, intuitively, my guys, my kids, they were kids, had to be in a empowered state. So I was always insisted on that. But the second thing I realized was that after I cracked the code for the straight line system and I had all these kids who had never been successful before, who didn't possess the skills to be successful, so it was almost the point, they didn't possess any special skills because you can't say, I don't want to be rich. Well, okay, what special skills do you have that would allow you to be rich? Because it's a competitive world, it's a very doggy dog world, right? Well, what happened was when I invented the straight line, we used to call it the great equalizer because it allowed kids who were basically disempowered on the skill scale, right? They had no great education behind them, no degrees, no family connections, and no special talents, but yet they could learn this, this ability to influence the perspective, this ultra-high level, and that equalized them with their well-educated counterparts, whether it be on Wall Street, Main Street, anywhere else, and because of that, it opened up a world of possibility where they couldn't achieve in the past, no matter how hard they tried, now they could. Tell a guy without the skill the straight line, I want you to go out there and sell and close and make the other thing, okay, great, I'll do it for a week. No one's buying, what's the point? Give them a specialized skill, like that where they actually can achieve. Well, then the problem becomes something very different. It becomes, okay, great, I just made $100,000 in three months. I don't think I'd make that in one year. What's the, I don't need to, that's more than I ever thought, why should I work harder? See, the standard for money and success is low. They never viewed themselves as being someone that was capable or worthy or had to and the operas had to, must make a million bucks or $2 million a year. So what happens is their standard, where they burn, where they feel okay is much lower, where someone like mine was, well, I always viewed myself as that guy that was going to be the most successful and so forth, so I had these beliefs behind it. So my standard was burning at 10, 50 million a year, whatever it was, right? Their standard was, if I make $100,000 a year, I will be on Easy Street. That caused me a problem because with the straight line system, you could make $100,000 a month. So what happens when you teach someone to make $100,000 a month by learning how to become a master closer, by learning how to manage their state so they can bring it to the table, right? Giving them an empowering belief system so they feel good about it, they go, but what happens if their standard says, I'm okay with the 100 grand, guess what? They stop working. They slow down. They won't keep going cause they say, you know what? What's up? I'm okay. And if they actually exceed that, they almost feel unnerved about it. Something is wrong. I don't have congruency, but if they have this amazing income, but my standard's not that much, so they will actually slow down even self-sabotage. I was keenly aware of that very early when I noticed that I would watch kids that would go out there and start making big money and they'd back off. So what I started doing, it's stratens, is out of necessity. I always say necessity is the mother of invention, right? I was forced to come up with ways to actually raise their standard. I had to do that because without that, they simply would have stopped working. Now admittedly, at the time, they didn't call that. I didn't really know what I was doing at an intuition. So I came up with strategies that were very powerful that allowed me to take one person or a thousand at a time and say things to them that would have them essentially rewrite their core belief systems about money and success. And as a result of that, they would actually raise their standard. And I would do it again and again and again. And what happened was you took these kids who thought they'd be okay making 100 grand and you'd see them three months later and say, if I don't make a million bucks a year, I'm a loser. That's raising someone's standard. So while I think that most people probably are not born with high or low, I think it's how you develop in adolescence when it comes into play. I think that in terms of money and success, many people transition into adulthood having standards for success that simply disentangling them. They were not raised in a family. They weren't told things that they could internalize and develop high standards for. So in those case, you have to intervene and raise those people's standards or else they will not perform at a level that they're capable of skill wise, belief wise, vision wise, intellectually wise, they won't stop because that's standards like that long-term barometer and that's that. All right, you're back now. Here's the deal. The important thing to remember about this lesson and why I thought it was so crucial to put it first is you get to see how all of these inner world competencies cross over with one another. There's no pure standards in the world without having belief systems and values. So when she asked me that first question, you know, where did all this start? It just seems you have this ability or pre-natural standard that's so high when it comes to money and success. Was that always the case? And as I thought that through, I'm like, well, it kind of started with beliefs and identity and then value and then standards kicked in afterwards, although they very quickly start to feed off one another and interrelate. In other words, you can develop a limiting belief that will in result drop your standard. I'm very careful here, which is why I made sure that this training overall included all aspects of the inner game that are relevant to success and sales. So what I want to do now is I want to stop. I want you to take a quiz, make sure you understand everything went over here. There'll be some exercise to start unlocking what your standard is. And as soon as you're done, meet me back in the next lesson as soon as you can. I'll see you there.
